title: referee run trace
description: One line of a --explain run trace. The file is newline-delimited JSON: a header, then the trace's signals, then one line per requirement. Validate line by line; a whole file is not a single document by design, so that a writer never holds the run in memory and a reader can stop early.

libraries.description: Objects loaded for external functions. Present so a verdict that depended on a binary says which one -- without it the run is not reproducible.

states.description: Written once. Every values array in the file is parallel to states.time, which is why this is not one line per state.

time.description: Timestamps in the trace's own unit, non-decreasing.

name.description: The @name, absent when the requirement is unnamed.

where.description: Source label, always present: it is the identity when there is no name. A trailing `.N` disambiguates requirements that share a source position.

vacuous.description: True when the requirement held for a reason unrelated to the system behaving. May be true together with verdict=pass -- that combination is the point.

scope.description: Omitted when the scope is not known. It must not be guessed: an empty `active` means the scope never opened, which is the vacuity signal, so writing it speculatively would mark every requirement vacuous.

witness.description: State index that settled the verdict, or null when nothing did.

rows.description: Absent or empty when only the verdict is known. Referee emits rows once it computes per-subexpression columns; until then a requirement line still says which requirement it is and what it decided, which is what the report shows.

allOf.description: A vacuity reason is required exactly when vacuous is true.

kind.description: bare is a requirement written without a scope, and so evaluated only at the first state.

active.description: Half-open [start, end) state-index pairs where the scope was open. An empty array is a scope that never opened -- the picture of vacuity, and why a reader needs to compute nothing.

kind.description: How the value must be drawn. state is a fact about that state. temporal is a claim about the suffix (or prefix) from it, and carries witnesses so it can be drawn as a span rather than a cell -- drawn as a cell it reads identically to a state formula, which is the one thing a viewer must not get wrong. window is an accumulator, and carries the window it summarised.

witnesses.description: temporal rows only. witnesses[i] is the state that settled values[i], or null when nothing did -- which for a strong operator is itself why it is false.

windows.description: window rows only. The [lo, hi) state range each value summarised.

ref.description: The id of a signal line, when this row is a signal the requirement reads. Carries no values of its own -- exactly one of ref or values is present.

allOf.description: A row either references a signal or carries its own values, never both and never neither.
at is present exactly when the encoding is sparse.

signal.description: One declared signal of the trace, written once. Requirement rows reference these by id rather than repeating them: a signal read by twenty requirements would otherwise appear twenty times. It also means a viewer can draw the trace itself, and signals a requirement never mentions -- which is most of why a timing diagram is worth looking at, since the correlation you did not predict is the one you needed.

computed.description: True for `data x = expr`, which is a property of the specification rather than of the recording.

allOf.description: at is present exactly when the encoding is sparse.

values.description: Dense: parallel to header.states.time, one entry per state. null means not evaluated here -- outside a scope, or short-circuited -- and is not the same as false. Conflating them is how a vacuous requirement comes to look satisfied.

at.description: Sparse: at[i] is the state index where values[i] takes effect, holding until the next entry. Strictly increasing, and at[0] is 0 -- a signal has a value from the first state. A change to null is an entry like any other, so 'unchanged' and 'not evaluated' stay distinguishable, which they would not be if absence meant both.

encoding.description: dense costs one entry per state; sparse costs one per *change*, plus its index. Which is cheaper depends entirely on the signal: a flag constant across a million states is one sparse entry and a million dense ones, while an octet that changes every state is cheaper dense than sparse, since sparse would carry an index alongside every value. The writer picks per signal by measuring; the reader handles both, which is a few lines. Forcing one encoding on data with these characteristics is what makes formats either bloated or slow.
